questions.question: What is the primary purpose of Audit Logs in Temporal Cloud?

choices.text: To record every Workflow execution, including starts, completions, and terminations inside a Namespace
To track administrative control plane operations — such as creating users or namespaces — answering who did what and when
To store Activity retry attempts and failure reasons so developers can debug worker crashes
To stream real-time metrics about Task Queue throughput and latency to an external monitoring tool

questions.explanation: Audit Logs capture control plane events — things like creating or deleting users, namespaces, API keys, and Nexus endpoints. They answer the 'who, when, and what' questions needed for security reviews and compliance. Notably, they do NOT capture data plane events like Workflow starts or terminations; the Export feature is used for that purpose instead.

questions.question: Which AWS service does Temporal Cloud use as the destination when you configure an Audit Log sink for AWS?

choices.text: Amazon SQS, a managed message queue service for decoupling distributed systems
Amazon CloudWatch, a monitoring service for collecting logs and metrics from AWS resources
Amazon Kinesis Data Streams, a real-time data streaming service for ingesting log records
Amazon DynamoDB, a fully managed NoSQL database for storing structured event data

questions.explanation: Temporal Cloud delivers Audit Logs to an Amazon Kinesis Data Stream that you own. You provide the Kinesis ARN when setting up the sink, and Temporal writes audit records directly to that stream. From there, it is your responsibility to build a consumer — for example, forwarding the records to S3 or a SIEM tool. Kinesis is chosen because it is designed for high-throughput, real-time data ingestion.

questions.question: Which role is required to configure an Audit Log Integration in Temporal Cloud?

choices.text: Any authenticated user in the account, because audit visibility is a transparency feature open to everyone
A Namespace Administrator, because audit logs are scoped to individual Namespaces
A Read-Only Viewer, because configuring the sink only requires read access to existing settings
An Account Owner or Global Administrator, because audit log configuration is an account-level administrative action

questions.explanation: Configuring an Audit Log Integration — or even viewing Audit Logs through the UI or API — requires the Account Owner or Global Administrator role. This makes sense because audit logs cover account-wide operations and contain sensitive security information. Namespace-level roles are not sufficient for this task.

questions.question: A security engineer asks: 'Which Temporal Cloud events are captured by Audit Logs?' Which statement correctly describes the scope of Audit Log coverage?

choices.text: Audit Logs capture both control plane operations (like CreateNamespace) and data plane operations (like Workflow Start and Schedule Create) to give a complete picture of all activity.
Audit Logs capture only data plane events such as Workflow Start, Workflow Terminate, and Schedule Create, while control plane changes like namespace updates must be retrieved via the Export feature.

Audit Logs capture control plane operations like CreateNamespace, DeleteUser, and CreateAPIKey, but do NOT capture data plane events such as Workflow Start or Schedule Create. The Export feature is used for closed Workflow Histories.
Audit Logs capture control plane operations and data plane events, but data plane events are only available for the past 7 days, while control plane events are retained for 30 days.

questions.explanation: Audit Logs are strictly limited to control plane operations — things like user management, namespace lifecycle, API key creation, and Nexus endpoint changes. Data plane events like Workflow Start, Workflow Terminate, and Schedule Create are explicitly NOT captured by Audit Logs. For closed Workflow Histories, you should use the Export feature instead. Option A is wrong because data plane events are excluded. Option B has the relationship backwards — Export is for Workflow Histories, not control plane changes. Option D invents a 7-day/30-day split that does not exist; the retention period is uniformly up to 30 days for Audit Logs.

questions.question: Your team is reviewing the Audit Log JSON format and notices several fields marked as deprecated. Which of the following correctly describes a deprecation and its replacement?

choices.text: The `status` field is deprecated and replaced by `level`, which provides richer status information such as LOG_LEVEL_INFO and LOG_LEVEL_ERROR.
The `caller_ip_address` field is deprecated and replaced by `x_forwarded_for`, which supports a comma-separated list of IP addresses to account for proxies in the network path.
The `principal` field is deprecated and replaced by `user_email`, because `principal` only applies to service account principals and not human users.
The `raw_details` field is deprecated and replaced by `details`, which provides a more structured representation of the request payload.

questions.explanation: `caller_ip_address` is deprecated in favor of `x_forwarded_for`, which uses the standard X-Forwarded-For comma-separated format evaluated from last to first, allowing customers to identify the true caller IP even when proxies are in the network path. Option A is backwards — `level` is the deprecated field, replaced by `status`. Option C is wrong; `principal` is the current field and `user_email` is the deprecated one (it is replaced by `principal.name` for user-type principals). Option D is also backwards — `details` is deprecated and replaced by `raw_details`.

questions.question: An operator wants to set up Audit Logs for their Temporal Cloud account using AWS Kinesis. Which IAM role and permission requirements does Temporal Cloud impose?

choices.text: Temporal Cloud requires the operator to create an IAM user (not a role) with programmatic access keys, and those keys are entered directly into the Temporal Cloud UI to authorize writes to the Kinesis stream.
Temporal Cloud writes to Kinesis using its own built-in AWS account, so no IAM role or cross-account permission setup is required from the operator's side.
Temporal Cloud requires the operator to bind a Kinesis Data Stream ARN to a Temporal-managed service principal; no IAM role is needed because Temporal Cloud uses OIDC federation automatically.

The operator must create an IAM role with write permission to the Kinesis stream in their AWS account. A CloudFormation template (provided by Temporal or generated in the UI) is used to create this role and grant Temporal cross-account access.

questions.explanation: Setting up the Audit Log sink with AWS Kinesis requires the operator to create an IAM role in their own AWS account that has write access to the Kinesis stream. Temporal Cloud provides a CloudFormation template (either via the Auto method, which launches the stack directly from the UI, or the Manual method, where you download and apply the template yourself) to automate this role creation and establish the cross-account trust. Option A is wrong because IAM roles are used, not IAM user access keys. Option B is wrong because Temporal does not have built-in access to the operator's AWS account. Option C invents an OIDC federation mechanism that is not described in the documentation.

questions.question: A compliance officer needs to retrieve Audit Logs for events that occurred 25 days ago. What is the correct approach, and what constraints apply?

choices.text: Audit Logs older than 7 days can only be retrieved by filing a support ticket; the Cloud UI and API only expose the past 7 days of events by default.
The Temporal Cloud Ops API supports retrieving Audit Logs up to 30 days in the past, with a maximum page size of 1000 events per request. The UI can also download up to 1000 events locally.
Audit Logs are retained indefinitely in Temporal Cloud storage; the 30-day figure only applies to the Kinesis stream, not to logs accessible through the UI or API.
Audit Logs beyond 24 hours can only be accessed if an active Kinesis or Pub/Sub sink was configured at the time the event occurred; historical events are not backfilled to the API.

questions.explanation: The Temporal Cloud Ops API allows access to Audit Logs for the past 30 days, using optional filters for start time, end time, page size (max 1000, default 100), and a page token for pagination. The Temporal Cloud UI also lets you download up to 1000 events to a local file. For events beyond 30 days, you need to file a support request. Option A invents a 7-day limit that does not exist. Option C is wrong — 30 days is the retention limit for all Audit Log access, not just Kinesis. Option D is wrong; the API can retrieve historical logs regardless of whether a streaming sink was active, up to the 30-day retention window.

questions.question: Your security team is reviewing Temporal Cloud Audit Logs to investigate a potential insider threat. They notice that several critical workflow operations — including Workflow Start, Workflow Terminate, and Schedule Create — are missing from the Audit Log stream. A junior engineer insists the Audit Log integration must be broken. What is the actual explanation, and what should the team use instead to capture those events?

choices.text: The Audit Log sink has a 10-minute delay, so these events will appear shortly. No additional tooling is needed; the team should simply wait and re-query the stream.
Kinesis rate limiting (1,000 messages/second) is silently dropping data-plane events. Upgrading to a higher-throughput Kinesis shard configuration will restore those missing entries.

Audit Logs intentionally capture only control-plane events (e.g., namespace changes, user management, API key operations). Data-plane events like Workflow Start or Terminate are not included; the Export feature should be used to send closed Workflow Histories to external storage instead.
These events require the Account Owner role to view. The current user only has Global Administrator privileges, which restricts access to data-plane Audit Log entries in the Kinesis stream.

questions.explanation: Audit Logs in Temporal Cloud are explicitly scoped to control-plane operations — things like CreateNamespace, DeleteUser, CreateAPIKey, and Nexus endpoint changes. They answer 'who changed what configuration, and when' for compliance purposes. Data-plane events such as Workflow Start, Workflow Terminate, and Schedule Create are deliberately excluded. If you need to capture workflow execution history for forensic or compliance purposes, the correct tool is the Export feature, which ships closed Workflow Histories to an S3 bucket or GCP storage. This is a common misconception: engineers assume Audit Logs cover all Temporal activity, but they only cover the control plane. Confusing the two leads to gaps in security investigations.

questions.question: A platform team configures a Temporal Cloud Audit Log sink targeting AWS Kinesis and hits the Verify button — it returns Success. Two weeks later, during a compliance audit, they discover that roughly 3% of audit entries are missing from downstream dashboards built on top of the Kinesis stream. The Kinesis shard is receiving data, but some records are being silently dropped before reaching the dashboard. Which operational reality of the Kinesis integration most directly explains this, and what must the team implement?

choices.text: Temporal retains Audit Logs for only 7 days; any records older than that are automatically purged from the stream before the consumer can read them, so the team must increase retention in the Temporal Cloud settings.

Kinesis enforces a rate limit of 1,000 messages per second and has quotas on both record count and record size. When these limits are exceeded, records are throttled and dropped. The team must implement their own consumer with retry logic and monitor shard-level throttling metrics.

The Verify button only confirms that Temporal can write one test record; it does not validate throughput capacity. The missing 3% is caused by the IAM role expiring after 14 days, which requires periodic rotation via CloudFormation stack updates.

The Audit Log sink serializes entries as Protobuf, but the dashboard consumer expects JSON. The 3% loss is due to deserialization failures on records that contain non-ASCII characters in the principal name field.

questions.explanation: Kinesis Data Streams imposes a hard rate limit of 1,000 messages per second per stream, plus quotas on total records written and record payload sizes. Temporal's Audit Log documentation explicitly calls this out as a known constraint. When control-plane activity spikes (e.g., a bulk user provisioning script, a namespace migration), the stream can be throttled and records dropped. The Verify button only confirms write access — it writes one record and checks for success, not sustained throughput. The team must build their own Kinesis consumer (as shown in the Go example in the docs) with proper error handling, and they should monitor CloudWatch for GetRecords.IteratorAgeMilliseconds and WriteProvisionedThroughputExceeded metrics. Temporal retains Audit Log data for 30 days (not 7), and the IAM role does not auto-expire after 14 days.

questions.question: An ops engineer is writing a SIEM integration that parses Temporal Cloud Audit Log entries. Their parser extracts the `user_email`, `caller_ip_address`, `level`, and `category` fields to populate dashboard panels. After a planned Temporal Cloud upgrade in early 2026, the dashboard panels start showing null values. What is the root cause, and which fields should the parser have used instead?

choices.text: The Audit Log format changed from JSON to Protobuf in the upgrade. The parser must be updated to deserialize Protobuf binary payloads and re-map all field names using the published proto schema.

The `user_email`, `caller_ip_address`, `level`, and `category` fields are all marked deprecated and scheduled for removal on or after April 1, 2026. The parser should use `principal.name`, `x_forwarded_for`, `status`, and omit `category` (which is no longer populated), respectively.

The Kinesis shard ARN changed during the upgrade, causing the stream to reconnect with a new iterator position. The engineer must re-configure the sink with the new ARN and backfill the missing 30 days via the Cloud Ops API.

These fields were moved into the nested `raw_details` object in version 2 of the log format. The parser needs to extract them from `raw_details.user_email` and `raw_details.caller_ip_address` rather than the top-level object.

questions.explanation: The Temporal Cloud Audit Log documentation includes an explicit deprecation notice: `user_email`, `caller_ip_address`, `level`, and `category` are all planned for removal on or after April 1, 2026. Their replacements are: `principal.name` for the user identity (noting that `user_email` only worked for `user`-type principals anyway), `x_forwarded_for` for the caller IP (which also handles proxy chains via comma-separated format), `status` instead of `level`, and `category` simply has no replacement because it is no longer used. A SIEM parser built on deprecated fields will silently return nulls after removal. This is a real operational risk: integrations that were written against an early version of the log format need proactive updates before the deprecation deadline.

questions.question: A compliance team wants to use the Temporal Cloud Ops API to build a real-time audit dashboard that pages on-call engineers when certain admin operations (like DeleteNamespace or DeleteUser) occur. They query the API every 5 minutes with no time filters. After a month, the dashboard starts missing events that occurred more than 30 days ago, and the on-call engineer asks whether they can recover those events by increasing the PageSize parameter. What is the correct assessment?

choices.text: Increasing PageSize above 1000 via a special admin API key will extend the accessible window to 90 days. The team should request elevated API permissions from Temporal Support to unlock this parameter.

The 30-day retention limit is a hard boundary for the Ops API, and PageSize cannot exceed 1,000. Events older than 30 days are permanently inaccessible via the API unless Temporal Support retrieves them from internal retention. For ongoing needs, the team should use an Audit Log sink so every event is captured as it is generated.

The API returns events in newest-first order, so increasing PageSize to 1,000 and paginating through all results with the PageToken will surface the older events that are being skipped by the default 100-record page size.

The missing events are within the 30-day window but are filtered out because the API defaults EndTimeExclusive to 30 days ago rather than the current time. Setting EndTimeExclusive explicitly to now will restore all missing events.

questions.explanation: The Temporal Cloud Ops API has two hard constraints relevant here: (1) it only surfaces Audit Logs from the past 30 days — events older than that are inaccessible via the API, and (2) PageSize cannot exceed 1,000 (defaulting to 100). Neither constraint can be worked around by the caller. The documentation explicitly states that to retrieve logs older than 30 days you must file a support request, and that the recommended pattern for real-time or comprehensive audit capture is an Audit Log sink (Kinesis or Pub/Sub), not the API. The API is suited for building dashboards over recent history; the sink pattern is suited for durable, every-event capture. The team's real mistake was relying solely on the API poll instead of also configuring a sink — by the time they query, events older than 30 days are already gone.

questions.question: A security team is building a compliance dashboard that must detect unauthorized namespace deletions and API key rotations within minutes of occurrence. They configure an Audit Log sink to AWS Kinesis, then write a Lambda consumer that reads from the stream and alerts on `DeleteNamespaceAPI` and `DeleteAPIKey` operations. During a drill, the team also wants to verify that a workflow termination fired by an operator shows up in the Audit Log stream. After the drill, none of the workflow termination events appear in Kinesis, but the namespace and API key events do. What is the most accurate explanation for this behavior, and what architectural change correctly addresses the missing workflow termination events?

choices.text: Workflow termination events are batched and delayed by up to 30 minutes before appearing in the Kinesis stream due to Temporal Cloud's deduplication window; increasing the Kinesis shard count will reduce the delay and surface the events within the SLA.

Audit Logs only capture control-plane operations on Temporal Cloud resources (namespace CRUD, API key lifecycle, user management, Nexus endpoints, etc.) and explicitly do not capture data-plane events like Workflow Terminate. The team must configure a separate Namespace Export sink to capture closed Workflow Histories and consume that for data-plane forensics.

Workflow termination events are captured in Audit Logs but only appear when the terminating caller authenticates with an API key rather than mTLS; switching the operator tooling to API-key-based auth will cause the `WorkflowTerminate` events to flow into the Kinesis stream.

The IAM role provisioned via CloudFormation lacks the `kinesis:PutRecords` permission for data-plane event categories; adding an explicit allow policy for `LOG_CATEGORY_DATAPLANE` in the IAM role trust policy will surface the missing workflow termination entries.

questions.explanation:
Temporal Cloud Audit Logs are explicitly scoped to the control plane — they record 'who, when, and what' for operations on Temporal Cloud resources such as namespaces, API keys, users, service accounts, connectivity rules, and Nexus endpoints. The documentation is unambiguous: 'Audit Logs do NOT capture data plane events, like Workflow Start, Workflow Terminate, Schedule Create, etc.' This is a fundamental architectural boundary, not a configuration or permissions gap. The Kinesis sink is working correctly for the control-plane events it is designed to carry.

For data-plane forensics, Temporal Cloud provides the Export feature, which ships closed Workflow Histories to external storage (S3 or GCS). These two pipelines serve different purposes: Audit Logs answer compliance questions about who administered the platform, while Export answers operational questions about what workflows executed and how they ended.

The production insight here is critical for compliance architects: a single Kinesis sink will never surface workflow-level events no matter how it is configured, because those events are never written to the Audit Log stream in the first place. Teams building SOC 2 or HIPAA audit trails need to understand that they require both pipelines — Audit Logs for control-plane accountability and Namespace Export for workflow-execution evidence — and must design their consumer infrastructure, retention policies, and alerting rules separately for each. Conflating the two will leave systematic gaps in compliance coverage that won't be discovered until an actual audit.

questions.question: An organization is migrating its Temporal Cloud Audit Log integration from the deprecated field schema to the v2 schema in preparation for the April 2026 deprecation deadline. Their existing consumer Lambda parses the `caller_ip_address` field to enforce a geo-IP allowlist policy: any admin operation originating from outside approved CIDR ranges triggers an automated account lockdown. After testing the new schema, the security team discovers that `x_forwarded_for` sometimes contains a comma-separated list of multiple IP addresses rather than a single IP. Their naive migration — replacing `caller_ip_address` with the first value in `x_forwarded_for` — incorrectly allows some operations from unapproved regions to pass the allowlist check. What is the correct parsing strategy for `x_forwarded_for` to accurately identify the true originating caller IP?

choices.text: The first IP in the comma-separated `x_forwarded_for` list always represents the true client origin because load balancers prepend the original caller before any proxies append their addresses; taking the first entry is the correct and complete migration from `caller_ip_address`.

The `x_forwarded_for` list must be evaluated from last to first, stopping at the first untrusted IP address encountered, because proxies in the network path append their own addresses at the end; the first untrusted IP working backward from the right is the true caller origin, not the leftmost entry.

Temporal Cloud guarantees that `x_forwarded_for` contains exactly one IP address in all cases where the caller reaches Temporal Cloud directly; multiple IPs only appear for service-account-initiated operations and can be safely deduplicated by taking any single element from the list.

The correct approach is to discard `x_forwarded_for` entirely and use the `principal.id` field combined with a separate IP lookup via the Cloud Ops API, because `x_forwarded_for` is not reliable for geo-IP enforcement when proxies are involved and is only provided for informational purposes.

questions.explanation:
The Temporal Cloud Audit Log documentation explicitly addresses this parsing challenge: 'The X-Forwarded-For format is a comma-separated list of IP addresses which should be evaluated from the last to the first, until meeting the first untrusted IP address of the list. This allows for instance to consider proxies in the path. Temporal provides the caller IP address in that format to allow customers to identify a caller IP address even if one (or more proxies) are in the network path to reach Temporal Cloud.'

The X-Forwarded-For header is appended to by each proxy that handles the request. A client at IP A passes through proxy P1 (trusted) and P2 (trusted) before reaching Temporal Cloud. The resulting header might look like: `10.1.2.3, 172.16.0.1, 192.168.1.1`. To find the true originating IP, you walk from right to left, skipping IPs belonging to known trusted proxies, and stop at the first IP you cannot verify as a trusted relay. Taking the leftmost (first) value is the classic XFF spoofing vulnerability — a malicious actor can prepend any IP they want to the front of the header before sending the request, making the first entry completely untrustworthy without additional validation.

The production implication is significant: geo-IP allowlist enforcement based on naively taking `x_forwarded_for[0]` can be trivially bypassed by prepending a trusted CIDR IP to the header. Security consumers of Audit Logs must implement the right-to-left traversal algorithm and maintain an explicit list of trusted proxy CIDRs. This is a well-known web security property (RFC 7239 discusses it), but it frequently trips up teams migrating from a single-IP field to an XFF-style field without reading the semantics carefully.
